data.title: Alerus Financial Non-GAAP EPS of $0.85 beats by $0.28
data.description:
• Alerus Financial press release (ALRS): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.85 beats by $0.28.

data.title: CopperCorp enters marketing services agreement
data.description:
• CopperCorp Resources (CPER:CA) said on Wednesday it entered a consulting services agreement with Capital Analytica on Jan. 27, 2026, granting options for 0.2M common shares at $0.10 each, exercisable over three years.
  • The company paid $0.075M on signing a six-month deal with Capital Analytica and $0.075M by April 27, 2026, and has an option to extend for six months at $0.075M.
  • Jeff French, arms-length to the company, is the principal of Capital Analytica and will oversee all services under the agreement.

data.title: Liberty Energy Non-GAAP EPS of $0.15 beats by $0.22, revenue of $4B beats by $150M
data.description:
• Liberty Energy press release (LBRT): FY Non-GAAP EPS of $0.15 beats by $0.22.
  • Revenue of $4B (-7.2% Y/Y) beats by $150M.
  • Fourth quarter 2025 revenue of $1.0 billion and net income of $14 million, or $0.08 fully diluted earnings per share
  • Fourth quarter 2025 Adjusted EBITDA1 of $158 million
  • Raised quarterly cash dividend by 13% to $0.09 per share beginning in the fourth quarter of 2025
  • Shares -1.83% AH.

data.title: Oil States signs new $125M credit agreement
data.description:
• Oil States International (NYSE:OIS) announced on Wednesday that it entered into a new credit agreement providing total commitments of $125M, including a $75M revolving facility and a $50M multi-draw term loan available through July 28, 2026.
  • The maturity date of the Cash Flow Credit Agreement is January 28, 2030.
  • The company said it bought back $50M of its 4.75% convertible senior notes due 2026 in Q4 2025, and $53M remained outstanding at year-end.
  • The company plans to retire the remaining notes using cash and credit facility borrowings, with cash totaling $70M and no borrowings outstanding.
  • Shares -2.33%

data.title: Hexcel beats Q4 estimates, lifts dividend as aerospace demand improves
data.description:
Shares of Hexcel (HXL) rose 2.2% in extended trading Wednesday after the aerospace materials maker reported fourth-quarter earnings and revenue that topped Wall Street expectations and announced a higher quarterly dividend.

Hexcel (HXL) posted sales of $491 million, exceeding analyst estimates of about $482 million for the three-month period. Adjusted earnings came in at $0.52 a share, ahead of the consensus forecast of $0.49 a share.

 

 

Net income rose to $46.4 million, or $0.60 a share, from $5.8 million, or $0.07 a share, in the year-earlier quarter.

Commercial aerospace trends improve

Hexcel (HXL) said fourth-quarter performance reflected improving conditions in commercial aerospace, particularly on the Airbus A320neo program, along with higher build rates on Boeing platforms. Commercial aerospace revenue increased 7.6% year over year, while lingering destocking continued to weigh on Airbus A350 sales.

Defense, space and other revenue declined modestly in the quarter, reflecting the divestment of an industrial business, partially offset by strength in military helicopter and space programs.

“Although 2025 was another challenging year for commercial aircraft production, we began to see positive trends in the fourth quarter that suggest a stronger 2026,” Chairman, Chief Executive Officer and President Tom Gentile said in a statement.

Margins and profitability

Adjusted operating income rose to $65.1 million, or 13.3% of sales, compared with $57.1 million, or 12.1% of sales, a year earlier. Gross margin declined slightly to 24.6% due to foreign exchange headwinds and restructuring costs.

Dividend increase and outlook

Hexcel (HXL) said its board approved a 6% increase in the quarterly dividend to $0.18 a share, payable Feb. 17 to shareholders of record Feb. 9.

For 2026, Hexcel (HXL) forecast sales of $2.0 billion to $2.1 billion, adjusted earnings per share of $2.10 to $2.30, compared with the analyst estimate of $2.27, and free cash flow of more than $195 million, as commercial aerospace recovery and higher defense spending support growth.

data.title: Meta in charts: R&D expenses top $17B in Q4; ARPP rises 16% Y/Y
Robinhood said to vie for role in SpaceX's giant IPO
data.description:
Robinhood Markets (HOOD), the upstart stock-trading app, is seeking a prime role in SpaceX's (SPACE) long-awaited initial public offering, according to a media report on Wednesday.

The company, known for gamifying stock trading, is vying with several Wall Street banks to grab a chunk of SpaceX stock to sell directly to its retail investors, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter. If successful, the company is expected to sell the shares through its IPO Access platform, which gives users the opportunity to buy stock at the IPO price before they trade in the open market, the article said.

That jibes with talk that SpaceX (SPACE) is considering setting aside a significant portion of shares for retail investors, as people have told Bloomberg.

Robinhood (HOOD) stock rose 0.8% in Wednesday after-hours trading.

Elon Musk's rocket and satellite company is seeking to raise more than $30B in an offering that would value the company at ~$1.5T, Bloomberg had reported. The company may aim for a June listing, some of the people said, which is around Musk's birthday. It may seek proceeds of as much as $50B, which would make it the biggest IPO ever.

Bank of America (BAC), Goldman Sachs Group (GS), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and Morgan Stanley (MS) are expected to get senior roles in the IPO.

data.title: Fair Isaac turns in Q1 profit beat as scores revenues shine; repeats guide
data.description:
Fair Isaac (FICO) shares dipped 2% in Wednesday after-hours trading even after the data analytics company delivered a double-beat on headline numbers for fiscal Q1 2026.

FICO also reiterated its FY2026 guidance for adjusted EPS of $38.17, vs. $40.70 consensus, and revenues of $2.35B, vs. $2.44B consensus.

Q1 adjusted EPS of $7.33, exceeding the $7.08 average analyst estimate, fell from $7.74 in the prior quarter but climbed from $5.79 a year ago.

Revenue of $512.0M for the three months ended Dec. 31, 2025, also beating the $501.9M consensus, compared with $515.8M for the quarter ended Sept. 30, 2025, and $440.0M for the quarter ended Dec. 31, 2024.

Scores revenues increased 29% Y/Y to $304.5M in Q1, driven by a higher mortgage origination scores unit price and an increase in the volume of mortgage originations. Meanwhile, software revenues edged up 2% Y/Y to $207.5M.

Total operating expenses totaled $277.9M in Q1, down from $278.6M in Q4 and up from $260.4M in the prior year's Q1.

data.title: First Commonwealth Financial signals 4% net interest margin target for 2026 while advancing share buybacks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Commonwealth Financial Corporation (FCF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Thomas Michael Price, President and CEO, reported "core EPS of $0.43 per share, which beat consensus earnings estimates alongside a net interest margin that expanded to 3.98%, a core ROA of 1.45% and a core efficiency ratio of 52.8%." He highlighted average deposit and total loan growth of 2.8% and 1.2%, respectively, during the quarter, with net interest income rising due to healthy new commercial loan volume at favorable rates.
  • Price noted a decrease in deposit costs to 1.83% and fee income remaining flat with gains in SBA offset by declines in wealth and mortgage. He emphasized a "concerted effort and long-term focus on growing fee income through our regional banking model."
  • The year saw core EPS of $1.53 and net interest income of $427.5 million, described by Price as benefiting from higher-for-longer interest rates, improved loan yields, lower deposit costs, and a better commercial business mix.
  • Loan growth for the year was 8.2% annualized and 5% excluding the Center Bank acquisition. Average deposit growth of 6.1% kept pace with loan growth, driven by money market, CDs, and noninterest-bearing DDA.
  • Price announced the retirement of Jane Grebenc, Bank President and Chief Revenue Officer, at the end of March, acknowledging her "pivotal role in the strategic transformations that have helped position First Commonwealth as a top quartile performer."
  • James Reske, CFO, stated, "Core operating results for the fourth quarter of 2025 continued the momentum of the third quarter. Core ROA improved 11 basis points to 1.45% and core ROTCE improved 93 basis points to 15.83%. Spread income increased $2.1 million from the previous quarter, primarily due to a 6 basis point increase in the net interest margin."
  • Reske noted the designation of a $225 million commercial loan portfolio as held for sale, representing a strategic exit from the Philadelphia MSA, with potential reinvestment into lower-yielding securities if the sale is completed.
  • Reske also highlighted that "core noninterest expense of $74.3 million increased $1.7 million from the previous quarter, mostly due to increases in salaries and benefits as we filled a number of open positions." He added, "remaining repurchase capacity under the current program was $22.7 million as of December 31, 2025. On top of that, an additional $25 million of share repurchase authority was authorized by our Board yesterday."

Outlook

  • NIM guidance remains largely unchanged, with Reske indicating "a near-term dip as our margin to our variable rate loans fully reflects fourth quarter rate cuts, followed by gradual improvement each quarter, ending the year 2026 at around 4%."
  • Noninterest income in 2026 is expected to be "relatively flat over 2025."
  • Reske expects to "limit operating cost increases to approximately 3% year-over-year looking ahead."
  • The sale of the Philadelphia loan portfolio, if consummated, is expected to improve liquidity and capital ratios but reinvestment will be at a rate differential of approximately 1.5%.

Financial Results

  • Core EPS for Q4 was $0.43 per share. Net interest margin expanded to 3.98%. Core ROA reached 1.45% and efficiency ratio was 52.8%. Deposit costs fell to 1.83%. Average deposits increased 2.8% annualized over last quarter. Provision for credit losses decreased by $4.3 million to $7 million compared to the previous quarter. The company repurchased $23.1 million of stock or 1.4 million shares at $15.94 per share in Q4, totaling 2.1 million shares in 2025.

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Questioned the outlook for net charge-offs and provisions in 2026, and updates on the dealer floor plan loan. Price responded, "The charge-off guidance we normally give is 25 to 30 basis points. And the floor plan credit, we have maybe $1.5 million left to resolve." Brian Sohocki, Chief Credit Officer, added, "We ended the year with a $2.5 million outstanding balance. So we're nearing resolution... There was no additional reserve as noted previously."
  • Tamayo: Asked about future loan sales. Price explained this was a one-off event, stating, "We really withdrew from that market... about 2 years ago, we sent customers letters, and this is kind of really one of the last acts of the play."
  • Karl Shepard, RBC: Asked about loan growth expectations. Price indicated, "I would expect that kind of loan growth to continue, although we really had elevated payoffs probably in excess of over $200 million from the second half of the year to the first half of the year."
  • Shepard: Inquired about buyback strategy. Reske said, "It's really more about capital deployment right now... if our future guidance is mid-single digits. We're generating far more capital than it takes to capitalize that kind of loan growth."
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Asked about margin sustainability and run rate. Reske responded, "We had some other things that offset that... previously on nonaccrual status. Some of the previously -- we recognized interest on those loans that have been on nonaccrual... looking ahead, we think that the Fed cut rates... That's not fully reflected yet. We're going to feel that in the variable... portfolio."
  • Motta: Questioned expense outlook. Price replied, "Efficiency was certainly on the back of the revenue side. We're normally very, very good at the expense side... we've invested pretty heavily in our commercial bank, our equipment finance group. And we expect more production there."
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: Probed on NIM guidance, spread compression, and pipeline yields. Price said, "When I look at our commercial variable and the new stuff we're putting on, it's 7.3% in the last quarter and commercial fixed is in the high 6s... replacement rates still look good." Reske added, "In the fourth quarter, we didn't see a real differential on the rates that were for the variable rate portfolio... not a lot of evidence on that macro level of spread compression."
  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: Asked about the impact of nonaccruals on NIM and the size of the expected dip in Q1. Reske stated, "The NPLs were uphold was about 3 basis points of total impact on the NIM... the amount of the dip, we think is anywhere from 5 to 10."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally positive but probing, particularly on credit quality, loan growth, and margin sustainability, with most questions aimed at understanding the durability of recent performance and the impacts of anticipated headwinds. Specific areas of skepticism included the sustainability of a 4% NIM and clarity on expense run rates.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing historical discipline and positive trends. Confidence was reflected in phrases such as "we feel like we're well positioned" and "we're generating so much capital."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained constructive but more focused on margin and credit risks, while management's confidence was consistent, though slightly more cautious in discussing future NIM and headwinds from loan sales and market exits.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for NIM in 2026 is now more explicit, targeting a 4% level by year-end, whereas last quarter the outlook centered around stability at approximately 3.9%.
  • Strategic focus shifted further from the Philadelphia market, with a $225 million commercial loan portfolio designated as held for sale.
  • Analysts’ questions in the current quarter centered more on the sustainability of margin improvements, effects of loan sales, and operating expense run rates, compared to the prior quarter's focus on deposit repricing and Center Bank integration.
  • Key metrics like core ROA, net interest margin, and efficiency ratio showed improvement versus the previous quarter. Management’s tone remained optimistic but added clarity on the challenges posed by payoffs and rate cuts.
  • Strategic priorities continue to center on regional growth, loan production, and expense discipline, with a new emphasis on capital deployment through share repurchases.

Risks and Concerns

  • Credit costs remain a watch area, with guidance for net charge-offs at 25 to 30 basis points and continued close monitoring of the remaining dealer floor plan exposure.
  • Elevated payoffs, particularly in commercial loan segments, present a headwind to loan growth.
  • Operating expense pressure from recent hiring and market wage dynamics was acknowledged, though management expects discipline to moderate increases.
  • Reinvestment of proceeds from the Philadelphia loan sale into lower-yielding securities may impact net interest income growth.
  • Analysts raised concerns on the sustainability of margin and potential spread compression, particularly in commercial real estate and C&I lending.

Final Takeaway

First Commonwealth Financial Corporation advanced its strategic focus on core profitability and capital deployment in the fourth quarter, recording improved operating metrics and authorizing additional share buybacks. Management’s guidance for a 4% NIM by the end of 2026, continued fee income stability, and disciplined operating cost increases reflect confidence in the bank’s ability to navigate rate and credit headwinds while shifting resources to higher-growth regional markets. The resolution of legacy credit exposures and ongoing focus on deposit growth and efficiency signal a disciplined approach to sustaining performance in the coming year.
